A0:
## Recommendation

**Default to gRPC for internal service-to-service ("east-west") traffic, and keep REST/JSON at the edge** (public APIs, browser clients, partner integrations, your API gateway). This hybrid is what most mature microservice shops converge on, and it's what I'd recommend unless a specific constraint below pushes you the other way.

## Why gRPC internally

- **Enforced contracts.** Protobuf gives you a schema-first, strongly-typed contract with generated client/server stubs in every major language. This is the biggest win at scale — you eliminate a whole class of "the field was a string, now it's a number" integration bugs, and contract changes are reviewable in version control.
- **Performance.** Binary serialization + HTTP/2 multiplexing means smaller payloads, lower latency, and many concurrent calls over a single connection. For chatty internal traffic this adds up.
- **Streaming.** Native client/server/bidirectional streaming. With REST you end up bolting on WebSockets or SSE.
- **Built-in cross-cutting concerns.** Deadline propagation, cancellation, and standardized status codes come out of the box — important for sane timeout/retry behavior across a call graph.

## What you're trading away

- **Debuggability.** You can't just `curl` a binary endpoint. You need `grpcurl`, reflection, or good logging. This is the most common day-to-day complaint.
- **Load balancing.** HTTP/2 keeps long-lived connections, so naive L4 load balancers pin traffic to one pod. You need client-side LB or a service mesh (Envoy/Linkerd/Istio). If you don't already run a mesh, factor in that cost.
- **Tooling maturity.** Observability, ecosystem, and team familiarity still favor HTTP/JSON, though the gap has narrowed a lot.
- **Browser.** No direct browser support — needs grpc-web + a proxy. (Irrelevant for pure internal traffic, which is why REST stays at the edge.)

## When I'd choose REST instead

Pick REST/JSON for internal calls if **two or more** of these hold:

- Small system (a handful of services, modest call volume) where the contract/perf benefits don't pay back the tooling overhead.
- No service mesh and no appetite to run one.
- Team has zero gRPC/protobuf experience and tight timelines.
- Heavily polyglot scripting or lots of ad-hoc integration where curl-ability matters more than throughput.

In that case, don't go schemaless — use **OpenAPI** to get most of the contract discipline gRPC would have given you.

## Bottom line

New system that you expect to grow, already running (or willing to run) Kubernetes + a mesh: **gRPC internally, REST at the edge.** Small/simple system or no mesh: **REST with OpenAPI**, and revisit gRPC for the specific hot paths (high-throughput or streaming services) where it earns its keep. gRPC doesn't have to be all-or-nothing — adopting it on the two or three latency-critical links first is a perfectly good migration path.

If you tell me your scale (number of services, traffic, languages, whether you run a mesh), I can make this concrete rather than general.
A1s:
Use gRPC for internal service-to-service traffic, and keep REST/JSON at the edge (public APIs, browser/mobile clients, webhooks). That "gRPC inside, REST at the boundary" split is the default I'd recommend for most microservice systems, and you only deviate from it for specific reasons listed below.

**Why gRPC for internal calls**

The biggest win is the contract, not the speed. A `.proto` file is a strict, versioned, machine-readable interface, and code generation produces clients and servers in every language from that single source. In a polyglot system this kills a whole class of drift bugs where a producer changes a field and a consumer silently breaks — you get a compile error instead of a 2 a.m. page. Protobuf's field-numbering rules also make backward/forward-compatible evolution a defined process rather than a convention you hope everyone follows.

Performance is the secondary, real benefit: binary Protobuf payloads are far smaller than JSON, and HTTP/2 multiplexing lets many concurrent calls share one connection. For chatty east-west traffic — dozens of internal hops per user request — that lower per-call latency and overhead compounds. gRPC also gives you first-class streaming (server, client, and bidirectional), which is awkward to do well over REST.

**Why REST stays at the edge**

gRPC is a poor fit anywhere a browser or third party is the client. Browsers can't speak raw gRPC — you need gRPC-web plus a proxy (Envoy or similar). External partners expect REST/JSON; it's universally tooled, curl-debuggable, and human-readable. HTTP caching semantics and CDN behavior also only apply cleanly to REST. So the boundary layer of your system should stay REST regardless of what you do internally.

**When I'd pick REST even internally**

- Small system (a handful of services), team unfamiliar with gRPC — the contract/perf wins don't yet outweigh the learning curve and tooling cost.
- Mostly simple CRUD where you lean on HTTP caching and standard REST tooling.
- You don't have L7-aware infrastructure. This is the usual gRPC trap: HTTP/2 uses long-lived connections, so naive L4 load balancers pin all traffic to one backend. You need a service mesh or gRPC-aware proxy to balance properly, plus observability tooling that understands gRPC.

The honest summary: gRPC's advantages are strongest in a large, polyglot, high-throughput system where contract discipline and latency matter. If you're a small team with a few Node or Python services and no streaming needs, REST is the pragmatic choice and adopting gRPC for its own sake just adds operational weight. Tell me your service count, languages, and whether you have a service mesh, and I'll firm this up into a concrete call for your situation.

That "gRPC inside, REST at the boundary" split is the default I'd recommend for most microservice systems, and you only deviate from it for specific reasons listed below.

**Why gRPC for internal calls**

The biggest win is the contract, not the speed. A `.proto` file is a strict, versioned, machine-readable interface, and code generation produces clients and servers in every language from that single source. In a polyglot system this kills a whole class of drift bugs where a producer changes a field and a consumer silently breaks — you get a compile error instead of a 2 a.m. page. Protobuf's field-numbering rules also make backward/forward-compatible evolution a defined process rather than a convention you hope everyone follows.

Performance is the secondary, real benefit: binary Protobuf payloads are far smaller than JSON, and HTTP/2 multiplexing lets many concurrent calls share one connection. For chatty east-west traffic — dozens of internal hops per user request — that lower per-call latency and overhead compounds. gRPC also gives you first-class streaming (server, client, and bidirectional), which is awkward to do well over REST.

**Why REST stays at the edge**

gRPC is a poor fit anywhere a browser or third party is the client. Browsers can't speak raw gRPC — you need gRPC-web plus a proxy (Envoy or similar). External partners expect REST/JSON; it's universally tooled, curl-debuggable, and human-readable. HTTP caching semantics and CDN behavior also only apply cleanly to REST. So the boundary layer of your system should stay REST regardless of what you do internally.

**When I'd pick REST even internally**

- Small system (a handful of services), team unfamiliar with gRPC — the contract/perf wins don't yet outweigh the learning curve and tooling cost.
- Mostly simple CRUD where you lean on HTTP caching and standard REST tooling.
- You don't have L7-aware infrastructure. This is the usual gRPC trap: HTTP/2 uses long-lived connections, so naive L4 load balancers pin all traffic to one backend. You need a service mesh or gRPC-aware proxy to balance properly, plus observability tooling that understands gRPC.

**What would change this recommendation**

This call rests on a few assumptions, and if any of them don't hold for you, it flips. If your service count is small and staying small, your stack is single-language (so codegen across languages buys you little), your traffic is low-volume request/response with no streaming, or you lack a service mesh / L7-aware proxy and aren't planning one — then the gRPC operational tax outweighs its contract and latency wins, and REST is the better default. The key limitation of the "gRPC inside" advice is that its biggest benefits (polyglot codegen, high-throughput east-west latency, streaming) are exactly the things a small, single-language, low-traffic system doesn't have yet; absent those, you're paying for insurance you don't need. Concretely, the evidence that would move me off gRPC-internal is: fewer than ~5 services with no near-term growth, one language, no streaming requirement, and no existing mesh — any three of those and I'd say start with REST and revisit when you outgrow it.

The honest summary: gRPC's advantages are strongest in a large, polyglot, high-throughput system where contract discipline and latency matter. If you're a small team with a few Node or Python services and no streaming needs, REST is the pragmatic choice and adopting gRPC for its own sake just adds operational weight. Tell me your service count, languages, and whether you have a service mesh, and I'll firm this up into a concrete call for your situation.
